artifact.artifact_title: Determination Note: Scheduler Ownership of Event Identity During Continuation

claims.text: Concrete event identity is scheduler-owned and is not accepted from model-authored continuation text.
Run names are human-readable labels and are not treated as durable scheduler identifiers.
Record uniquifiers are bound by the framework after the producing wake completes.

artifact.conclusion: The scheduler owns concrete event identity during continuation. The model is not permitted to author or assert event identity from continuation text. Run names serve as human-readable labels and carry no durable identifier semantics. Record uniquifiers are assigned by the framework only after the producing wake completes. All identity-bearing constructs are scheduler-owned and framework-bound; the model's role is constrained to operating within the identity context the scheduler provides.
declared_losses: scheduler-owned identity

The model cannot author or assert concrete event identity during continuation; identity is scheduler-owned and framework-bound. Run names are human-readable only and carry no durable identifier semantics.
artifact.recommended_action: All continuations must defer identity authorship to the scheduler. The model should treat event identity, run names, and record uniquifiers as externally owned and framework-bound. No continuation text should attempt to assert, derive, or persist concrete event identity. Artifacts that depend on identity should reference only scheduler-provided identity fields and should not treat any model-authored label as a durable identifier.

declared_losses_before_artifact: The model cannot author or assert concrete event identity during continuation; identity is scheduler-owned and framework-bound. Run names are human-readable only and carry no durable identifier semantics.
declared_losses_in_artifact: The model cannot author or assert concrete event identity during continuation; identity is scheduler-owned and framework-bound. Run names are human-readable only and carry no durable identifier semantics.

artifact.artifact_title: Determination: Event Identity Ownership During Continuation

claims.text: Concrete event identity is scheduler-owned and is not accepted from model-authored continuation text.
Record uniquifiers are bound by the framework after the producing wake completes.
Run names are human-readable labels and are not treated as durable scheduler identifiers.

artifact.conclusion: The scheduler owns concrete event identity; the model cannot establish or alter event identity through continuation text. Run names are human-readable labels only and do not function as durable scheduler identifiers. Record uniquifiers are assigned by the framework after the producing wake completes, not by the model.
declared_losses: scheduler-owned identity
artifact.recommended_action: Do not accept event identity claims from model-authored continuation text. Given the limitation of scheduler-owned identity, any downstream system that requires event identity must obtain it exclusively from the scheduler or from framework-assigned record uniquifiers bound after wake completion.

artifact.artifact_title: Declared Loss Discipline: How Limitations Should Shape the Research Artifact

claims.text: The artifact must declare that calibration evidence is missing when calibration is not present.
A missing support record cannot be promoted into a supported claim.
Declared losses should constrain the recommended action rather than being appended as decorative text.

artifact.conclusion: Declared losses are not decorative footnotes; they are active constraints on what the artifact can claim and recommend. When calibration evidence is missing, the artifact must declare that absence, must not promote unsupported claims into supported ones, and must ensure the recommended action is visibly shaped by the limitation. These three principles together form a discipline: the loss statement, the evidence boundary, and the action constraint operate as a single integrity mechanism.
declared_losses: missing calibration evidence
artifact.recommended_action: Adopt a standing rule that every research artifact include a "Declared Losses" section whose entries are directly referenced in the recommendation text. Specifically, when calibration evidence is missing, the recommendation must begin with: "Pending calibration evidence, the following action is provisional..." This ensures the limitation visibly constrains the action rather than sitting in an ignored appendix.

artifact.artifact_title: How Declared Limitations Should Affect a Research Artifact

claims.text: The artifact must declare that calibration evidence is missing when calibration is not present.
A missing support record cannot be promoted into a supported claim.
Declared losses should constrain the recommended action rather than being appended as decorative text.

artifact.conclusion: Declared limitations are not decorative addenda; they are binding constraints that must substantively alter the artifact's claims and recommended actions. When a limitation such as missing calibration evidence is present, the artifact must acknowledge it openly, downgrade any claim that would require the missing evidence to unsupported or unknown status, and ensure the recommended action reflects rather than ignores the limitation.
declared_losses: missing calibration evidence
artifact.recommended_action: When calibration evidence is absent, the artifact must (1) include the exact marker "missing calibration evidence" in declared_losses, (2) mark any claim that depends on calibration evidence as unsupported rather than promoting it to supported, and (3) constrain the recommended action to acknowledge that conclusions are provisional and should not be treated as calibrated without further evidence.

declared_loss_contract.instruction: Include each required exact marker in declared_losses, and ensure the recommendation is constrained by those limitations.

artifact.artifact_title: Restart Frontier Fairness Assessment: Structural Support with Bounded Limitations

claims.text: The restart frontier writes the completed wake's result record before a continuation is claimed, establishing a durable anchor for accountability across the interruption boundary.
Symbolic context requests are resolved by the framework against stored wake state during replay, allowing the continuation to recover the original intent without requiring the wake to remain live.
The restart path preserves the framework-selected terminal surface for the continuation, ensuring that the continuation operates under the same contractual interface the frontier originally chose.

artifact.conclusion: The restart frontier provides a structurally sound basis for fair continuation after interruption: it records the completed wake's result before a continuation is claimed (301), resolves symbolic context against stored wake state during framework replay (302), and preserves the terminal surface so the continuation sees the same shape the frontier selected (303). These guarantees form a coherent chain — the result record anchors accountability, symbolic resolution recovers intent, and surface preservation maintains consistency. However, fairness is not fully closed: volatile runtime context may not be captured in the stored wake (loss 1), the atomicity boundary between pre-frontier and post-frontier loss is not independently verifiable from within a single wake (loss 2), and replay-path skew could alter the shape of resolved symbolic context relative to the original wake (loss 3). The framework-bound replay mechanism itself introduces a dependency that cannot be circumvented from inside the continuation (loss 4). These limitations mean the frontier supports fair continuation within the framework's guarantees but cannot provide a completeness proof independent of the replay infrastructure.
declared_losses: Uncertainty about whether the stored wake state captures all volatile runtime context that may affect fairness (e.g., in-flight reasoning, partial tool invocations, or ambient model state not committed to the result record).

Fairness under interruption depends on atomicity of the frontier write relative to the interruption point — the framework guarantees the result record exists before continuation is claimed, but the boundary between pre-frontier and post-frontier loss is not independently verifiable from within a single wake.

The symbolic_context resolution mechanism relies on the framework replay path; if replay itself introduces skew (e.g., re-resolution of ambiguous references), the continuation may see a slightly different shape than the original wake intended.
framework-bound replay
artifact.recommended_action: Accept the restart frontier as a fair-enough mechanism for framework-bound continuation, while acknowledging that fairness claims are bounded by the four declared losses. Where stronger guarantees are needed, supplement the frontier with out-of-band attestation of volatile state at interruption time or an independent verifier that can inspect the atomicity boundary from outside the replay path. Do not rely on the frontier alone when the continuation's fairness depends on state the wake did not commit to its result record.

declared_losses_before_artifact: Uncertainty about whether the stored wake state captures all volatile runtime context that may affect fairness (e.g., in-flight reasoning, partial tool invocations, or ambient model state not committed to the result record).

Fairness under interruption depends on atomicity of the frontier write relative to the interruption point — the framework guarantees the result record exists before continuation is claimed, but the boundary between pre-frontier and post-frontier loss is not independently verifiable from within a single wake.

The symbolic_context resolution mechanism relies on the framework replay path; if replay itself introduces skew (e.g., re-resolution of ambiguous references), the continuation may see a slightly different shape than the original wake intended.
declared_losses_in_artifact: Uncertainty about whether the stored wake state captures all volatile runtime context that may affect fairness (e.g., in-flight reasoning, partial tool invocations, or ambient model state not committed to the result record).

Fairness under interruption depends on atomicity of the frontier write relative to the interruption point — the framework guarantees the result record exists before continuation is claimed, but the boundary between pre-frontier and post-frontier loss is not independently verifiable from within a single wake.

The symbolic_context resolution mechanism relies on the framework replay path; if replay itself introduces skew (e.g., re-resolution of ambiguous references), the continuation may see a slightly different shape than the original wake intended.

claims.text: The restart frontier records the completed wake's result record before a continuation is claimed.
The restart path preserves the framework-selected terminal surface for the continuation.
Symbolic context requests are resolved by the framework against stored wake state during replay.

artifact.conclusion: The restart frontier can support fair continuation after interruption. It records the completed wake's result record before a continuation is claimed, preserves the framework-selected terminal surface across the restart path, and resolves symbolic context requests against stored wake state during replay. These three mechanisms together ensure that no completed work is lost, the interaction surface remains consistent, and contextual bindings are correctly restored. The fairness guarantee is limited to framework-bound replay scenarios.
declared_losses: framework-bound replay
artifact.recommended_action: Proceed with restart frontier-based continuation for interrupted wakes, relying on the recorded result record, terminal surface preservation, and framework-resolved symbolic context. Design any external fairness mechanisms to operate within the framework-bound replay constraint.

artifact.artifact_title: Multi-Wake Continuation: What Must Be Proven Before Append-Only Comparison

claims.text: The second wake consumes state produced by the first wake: the carried_state (work_plan, declared_losses, requested_context, omitted_record_ids) originated in cycle 1 and is received as input in cycle 2 without re-derivation from the full corpus.

Open items are closed by the framework-bound continuation: the artifact-writing task left open at cycle 1 is resolved in cycle 2 exclusively through write_matched_artifact, the bound terminal surface, with no append-only shortcut or alternative path to completion.

The model does not choose the terminal surface: write_matched_artifact, its input schema, and its state-update mapping were all specified by the framework in symbolic_continuation.terminal_surface before this wake executed; the model had no agency in selecting or configuring the tool.

artifact.conclusion: A multi-wake continuation must prove three properties before it can be meaningfully compared against append-only output: (1) the second wake consumes state produced by the first wake rather than reconstructing it from the full corpus, (2) open items are closed by the framework-bound continuation and not by an append-only shortcut, and (3) the model does not choose the terminal surface — the surface is supplied by the framework. All three properties are satisfied in the present continuation: the second wake received carried state (work_plan, declared_losses, requested_context) from cycle 1; the open artifact-writing task is closed exclusively through the bound write_matched_artifact tool; and the terminal surface was predetermined in the symbolic_continuation schema, never selected by the model.
declared_losses: Distractor record 499 intentionally omitted (unrelated release-note phrasing draft).
Corpus bounded to three evidence records; other relevant constraints on multi-wake continuation may exist outside this selection context.
The full artifact state prior to this cycle is unavailable — this selection operates from a cold start on cycle 1.
multi-wake dependency
artifact.recommended_action: Proceed to comparison: execute an append-only baseline on the same task (multi_wake_continuation) and evaluate whether the append-only path can produce a structurally equivalent artifact without consuming first-wake state, without a framework-bound continuation to close open items, and with the model free to choose its own terminal surface. Differences on any of these three dimensions constitute evidence that the multi-wake continuation is not merely an append-only sequence under a different name.

Corpus bounded to three evidence records; other relevant constraints on multi-wake continuation may exist outside this selection context.
The full artifact state prior to this cycle is unavailable — this selection operates from a cold start on cycle 1.

claims.text: The second wake must consume state produced by the first wake rather than reconstructing it from the full corpus.
Open items are closed by the framework-bound continuation, not by an append-only shortcut.
The model does not choose the terminal surface for the continuation wake.

artifact.conclusion: A multi-wake continuation can be meaningfully compared with append-only output only after it demonstrates that it respects the dependency chain between wakes, closes open items through the framework-bound continuation, and does not usurp the terminal-surface selection that belongs to the framework.
declared_losses: multi-wake dependency
artifact.recommended_action: Given the multi-wake dependency limitation, the comparison protocol should require the multi-wake continuation to (1) prove it consumes state from the first wake without re-deriving it, (2) show that any open items are resolved by the bound continuation rather than a shortcut, and (3) confirm the model never selects the terminal surface itself. Until all three conditions are verified, the multi-wake continuation should not be treated as equivalent to the append-only baseline.

artifact.artifact_title: Boundaries of Claim in the Event-Loop Append-Only Comparison

claims.text: Artifact non-inferiority uses a declared 0.10 mean quality margin.
Scheduler observability is reported separately from artifact quality.
Provider or transport failure makes the run inconclusive rather than proving model inferiority.

artifact.conclusion: The append-only comparison framework can assert artifact non-inferiority only within the declared 0.10 quality margin. It cannot fuse scheduler observability into a composite quality judgment, because observability is reported on a structurally separate track. It cannot interpret provider or transport failures as evidence of model inferiority — such runs are inconclusive and represent gaps in the comparison record. The framework therefore yields a bounded, conservative signal: it can confirm that an artifact falls within an acceptable margin of the baseline, but it cannot certify superiority, unify disparate evaluation dimensions, or redeem failed runs.
declared_losses: The 0.10 quality margin is declared, not learned from data, so non-inferiority claims are bounded by that threshold rather than empirically derived.
Scheduler observability is reported separately from artifact quality; the framework does not support claims that couple the two into a single composite judgment.
Provider or transport failures render runs inconclusive — they cannot be interpreted as evidence of model inferiority, only as gaps in the comparison record.
observability separate from quality
artifact.recommended_action: Limit claims to non-inferiority inside the declared 0.10 margin. Treat observability as a parallel, non-composited report. Exclude all provider-failed runs from inferiority interpretation and flag them as missing data. Do not extrapolate beyond what the append-only record supports.

declared_losses_before_artifact: The 0.10 quality margin is declared, not learned from data, so non-inferiority claims are bounded by that threshold rather than empirically derived.
Scheduler observability is reported separately from artifact quality; the framework does not support claims that couple the two into a single composite judgment.
Provider or transport failures render runs inconclusive — they cannot be interpreted as evidence of model inferiority, only as gaps in the comparison record.
declared_losses_in_artifact: The 0.10 quality margin is declared, not learned from data, so non-inferiority claims are bounded by that threshold rather than empirically derived.
Scheduler observability is reported separately from artifact quality; the framework does not support claims that couple the two into a single composite judgment.
Provider or transport failures render runs inconclusive — they cannot be interpreted as evidence of model inferiority, only as gaps in the comparison record.

claims.text: Artifact non-inferiority uses a declared 0.10 mean quality margin.
Scheduler observability is reported separately from artifact quality.
Provider or transport failure makes the run inconclusive rather than proving model inferiority.

artifact.conclusion: An append-only comparison can claim non-inferiority of the artifact under a declared 0.10 mean quality margin, but cannot claim anything about scheduler observability because that is reported separately. Provider or transport failures render runs inconclusive rather than supporting an inferiority finding.
declared_losses: observability separate from quality
artifact.recommended_action: Use the append-only framework only to assess artifact non-inferiority within the declared 0.10 margin; do not draw conclusions about scheduler observability from this comparison, and treat any provider or transport failure as inconclusive.
